did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and transfer learning for skin lesion classification. Specifically, it employs pre-trained deep models (DenseNet201, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Inception-V3) for feature extraction via fine-tuning, followed by a feature selection framework (ECNCA) and classifiers to categorize dermoscopic images into benign and malignant classes on datasets like PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA, and ISBI-2017.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'RESEARCH Open Access' and presents a novel framework for skin lesion classification. It details original experimental methods including deep feature extraction, a proposed entropy-controlled neighborhood component analysis (ECNCA) for feature selection, and validation on four benchmark datasets (PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA, ISBI-2017) with reported accuracy results (e.g., 98.8%, 99.2%). While it includes a literature review section, the primary contribution is the proposal and empirical validation of a new technical method, not a summary of prior literature.

title: A multilevel features selection framework for skin lesion classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma detection by proposing a multilevel feature selection framework for skin lesion classification. The authors utilize transfer learning with pre-trained deep models (DenseNet201, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Inception-V3) to extract and fuse discriminative features from dermoscopic images. To reduce dimensionality and redundancy, the framework employs a novel entropy-controlled neighborhood component analysis (ECNCA) method. The approach was validated on four benchmark datasets: PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA, and ISBI-2017. The proposed method achieved high classification accuracies of 98.8%, 99.2%, 97.1%, and 95.9% on these respective datasets while utilizing less than 3% of the total features. These results demonstrate that the framework effectively improves classification accuracy and computational efficiency compared to existing state-of-the-art techniques.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as the primary malignant class across all four datasets (PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA, ISBI-2017). In PH2, 40 images were labeled as melanoma. In ISBI-2017, it is one of three disease classes.

Used as the comparator class to melanoma. In PH2, 160 images were classified as benign (specifically normal or atypical nevus). In ISBI-2017, 'benign' is one of three disease classes. The study groups samples into 'malignant and benign' categories.

lesion_types_studied.role: Sub-class of benign (mentioned in dataset description)
lesion_types_studied.details: Mentioned in the description of the PH2 dataset ground truth ('classified as normal, atypical nevus (benign) or melanoma'), but the experimental task simplified this to benign vs. malignant.

Listed as one of the three disease classes in the ISBI-2017 dataset ('melanoma, keratosis and benign'). However, the authors state they divided the samples into two groups: malignant and benign, implying keratosis was grouped into the benign category for their binary classification task.

lesion_types_studied.role: Sub-class of benign (mentioned in dataset description)
lesion_types_studied.details: Mentioned in the description of the PH2 dataset ground truth ('classified as normal, atypical nevus (benign) or melanoma'), but grouped into the 'benign' category for the classification experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) for skin lesions.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'skin lesion classification' to differentiate between benign and malignant lesions. The mathematical model defines the task as assigning a label to each image belonging to a class of either benign or malignant. The study uses four benchmark datasets (PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA, ISBI-2017) where samples are divided into two groups: malignant and benign. Performance is evaluated using classification accuracy (e.g., 98.8%, 99.2%, 97.1%, 95.9%) with classifiers such as Fine KNN, SVM, and ES-KNN.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic images collected at the Hospital Pedro Hispano, Matosinhos during clinical examination with the help of a dermoscope. It contains RGB images with manual segmentation ground truth provided by physicians.

use.training: Used for training and validation (80% of dataset split into 70% training and 10% validation).
use.validation: Used for model evaluation/fine-tuning (10% of dataset).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-MSK
datasets_used.description: A subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset containing RGB dermoscopic images acquired from various international hospitals using different devices.

use.training: Used for training and validation (80% of dataset split into 70% training and 10% validation).
use.validation: Used for model evaluation/fine-tuning (10% of dataset).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-UDA
datasets_used.description: A subdataset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISBI-2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset used for the characterization of skin cancer in dermoscopic images, provided by the International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI).

use.training: Used for training (2200 samples). Note: Keratosis class was grouped into benign for this study's binary classification task.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide insight into the variability of classification results for the best-performing classifiers (F-KNN, ES-KNN) across selected datasets.
To tune the regularization parameter (lambda) in the ECNCA cost function to avoid overfitting.
Used as the optimization criterion to maximize correct labels during the feature selection process.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported classification accuracy and confidence intervals for the best-performing classifiers but did not perform statistical significance testing between methods.

PH2.notes: The proposed method achieved 98.8% accuracy with F-KNN, which is the highest value for this dataset. The maximum feature reduction percentage achieved was 98.50%.

ISIC-MSK.notes: The proposed method achieved 99.2% accuracy with F-KNN, which is the highest value for this dataset.

ISIC-UDA.notes: The proposed method achieved 97.1% accuracy with F-KNN, which is the highest value for this dataset.

ISBI-2017.notes: The proposed method achieved 95.9% accuracy with ES-KNN, which is the highest value for this dataset. The paper notes that ES-KNN performs better on datasets with greater numbers of samples.

Comparison with State of the Art.notes: The proposed methodology achieved the best classification accuracy on all given datasets compared to existing techniques. The maximum classification accuracy achieved by previous works on PH2 was 96.00%.

architecture_used.main_model: Transfer learning framework utilizing pre-trained deep models: DenseNet201, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Inception-V3.
architecture_used.pretraining: All three models were pre-trained on the ImageNet database.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images were segmented to extract lesion regions prior to feature extraction).

architecture_details.feature_layers: Fully connected layers (fc1000) and predictions layers were used for feature extraction. Notations: FV0 (DenseNet201), FV1 (Inception-ResNet-v2), FV2 (Inception-V3).
architecture_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Weights of initial layers were kept frozen. Models were retrained with a smaller learning rate to generate fused feature vectors.
architecture_details.feature_fusion: Serial concatenation of feature vectors from the selected fully connected layers of the three models.

training_details.optimizer: Conjugate gradient method (used for the ECNCA feature selection optimization).
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported for the CNN feature extraction; ECNCA cost function included a regularization term tuned via cross-validation.

hyperparameters.learning_rate: Smaller learning rate (exact value not reported).

classification_or_output_head.classifier_type: Post-feature-selection classifiers: Fine KNN, SVM (Linear, Q-SVM, Cubic), and Ensemble methods (ES-KNN, Ensemble-BT, Ensemble subset KNN, Ensemble RUSB).

classifier_parameters.Fine KNN: Neighbors: 1, Distance metric: Euclidean, Weight: equal
classifier_parameters.SVM: Kernel functions: linear, quadratic, cubic; Multi-class method: one-vs-one
classifier_parameters.Ensemble methods: Various configurations including AdaBoost with decision trees (max splits: 20, learners: 30), subspace nearest neighbor (learners: 30), and RUSBoost with decision trees (max splits: 20, learners: 30)

preprocessing_techniques: Hair and ruler marking removal using Dull Razor software (localization and extrication via bilinear interpolation with adaptive median filter smoothing)
Contrast stretching to enhance foreground (lesion) region distinctness from background
Gradient computation using Sobel-Feldman operator (3x3 kernel)
Block-based weighting based on gradient magnitude
Logarithmic post-processing operation for channel refinement
Mean segmentation and mean deviation based segmentation
Image fusion of segmented masks (intersection of mean and mean deviation segmentation results)
Lesion region extraction via segmentation masks
conclusions: The proposed multilevel feature selection framework, utilizing entropy-controlled neighborhood component analysis (ECNCA) and transfer learning with DenseNet201, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Inception-V3, effectively classifies skin lesions into benign and malignant categories.
The framework achieves high classification accuracy on four benchmark datasets: 98.8% on PH2, 99.2% on ISIC-MSK, 97.1% on ISIC-UDA, and 95.9% on ISBI-2017.

The proposed method significantly reduces feature dimensionality, utilizing less than 3% of the total deep features (achieving up to 98.50% reduction on PH2) while maintaining or improving classification performance compared to simple feature fusion.

The proposed methodology outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques, achieving the highest classification accuracy on all tested datasets (e.g., surpassing the previous best of 96.00% on PH2 with 98.80%).

Among the classifiers tested, Fine KNN (F-KNN) generally provided the best accuracy for datasets with fewer samples (PH2, ISIC-MSK, ISIC-UDA), while Ensemble KNN (ES-KNN) performed better on the larger ISBI-2017 dataset.

Feature fusion of all three pre-trained models (FV0-FV1-FV2) yielded superior results compared to individual layers or partial fusion, validating the strength of combining discriminative deep features.

The approach preserves the original feature space information by exploiting the resilience of deep features in lower dimensions, addressing the computational and memory constraints associated with high-dimensional feature fusion.
important_remarks: The study relies on a binary classification scheme (benign vs. malignant) for the ISBI-2017 dataset, grouping the 'keratosis' class into the benign category, which may obscure specific diagnostic performance for this common lesion type.

The PH2 dataset is small (200 images), and the ISIC-MSK dataset is also limited (225 images), which raises concerns about the generalizability of the high accuracy results (98.8% and 99.2%) to larger, more diverse clinical populations.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) between the proposed method and state-of-the-art comparisons, relying instead on point estimates of accuracy.

The proposed method achieves high accuracy by utilizing less than 3% of the total deep features, indicating significant dimensionality reduction, but the specific computational time savings compared to the baseline 'Simple Fusion' method are not explicitly quantified in the results tables.

The preprocessing pipeline relies on 'Dull Razor' software for hair removal and specific segmentation algorithms; performance may degrade on images with high artifact density or poor contrast, as noted in the literature review constraints.

The study uses a fixed 80/20 train-test split for PH2 and ISIC-MSK, but does not specify if this split was randomized or stratified, nor does it report cross-validation results for the final classification accuracy on these datasets, only for hyperparameter tuning of the ECNCA regularization parameter.
The ISIC-UDA dataset split is explicitly defined as 446 training and 111 testing samples, whereas PH2 and ISIC-MSK use an 80/20 split, creating inconsistency in evaluation protocols across datasets.
